text: The surgeon enters the supravesicular space, performing gentle sweeping motions to stay on the flap. In the background, an indirect inguinal hernia is visible, with no contents observed upon insufflation in Trendelenburg position.
The surgeon uses scissors, a Maryland dissector, and a needle driver to perform lateral sweeping motions, carefully staying on the flap within the surgical field.

The surgeon carefully dissects around the hernia sac, avoiding the cord lipoma, which begins to come into view. The focus remains on the hernia sac to prevent distortion and avoid drifting into the spermatic cord.

The surgeon uses gentle cautery to release counter traction on the hernia sac, causing the surrounding tissue to fall away. The end of the sac is visible, with a significant amount of fat left behind, indicating the correct surgical plane.
The surgeon addresses the cord lipoma, improving visualization of its relation to the spermatic cord after the hernia sac has been taken down. The cord lipoma is seen flopping back onto itself.
The surgeon carefully manipulates the cord lipoma, keeping it elevated to avoid nearby nerves. The lipoma is held up, allowing for energy application if bleeding occurs.
The surgeon carefully teases the cord lipoma away from the spermatic cord and associated structures, using gentle manipulation to separate the lipoma from the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon creates a medial pocket, extending dissection past the symphysis pubis and below Cooper's ligament and the pubic tubercle.
The surgeon performs high lateral fixation above the myopectineal orifice using two ovicral sutures, positioning them close to the iliac vein to achieve a better curve on the mesh.
The surgeon identifies the indirect inguinal hernia sac and begins suturing it back onto the flap to prevent complications such as internal hernias or bowel obstructions.

The surgeon ligates the hernia sac at its base, noting the potential for a robust blood supply that can lead to increased bleeding and hematomas. The decision is made to suture the hernia sac back onto the flap to mitigate these risks.

The surgeon assesses the reach of the flap and sutures one side of it, anchoring it back to the flap. The suture is carried down to the deep internal ring, ensuring that the flap is securely positioned to prevent any sliding that could lead to an internal hernia.
